rubric_en: Why reactionaries are taking over the world
rubric_zh: 为何反动派正在接管世界

paragraphs.en: This article was featured in the One Story to Read Today newsletter. Sign up for it here.
paragraphs.zh: 这篇文章曾刊登在《今日一篇故事》时事通讯中。请在此订阅。

paragraphs.en: Maybe you’ve seen photos of Tehran in the 1970s, just before the Islamic Revolution: images of young women going to work in miniskirts, of couples making out in parks while wearing bell-bottoms, of people at pools in bikinis. It looks like Paris or Milan or Los Angeles. But in 1979 the revolution happened, and now Tehran looks like something from an earlier century.
paragraphs.zh: 也许你看过1970年代德黑兰的照片，那时正值伊斯兰革命前夕：年轻女性穿着迷你裙去上班，情侣在公园里戴着喇叭裤牵手亲吻，人们在泳池里穿比基尼。它看起来像巴黎、米兰或洛杉矶。但1979年革命发生后，德黑兰现在看起来更像前几个世纪的景象。

paragraphs.en: Sometimes I think that our whole world has become kind of like that—going backwards in time. The religious movements thriving in today’s secularized age are the traditionalist ones that dissent from large parts of contemporary culture—not only the Shiite Islam of post-revolution Iran, but Orthodox Judaism and conservative Catholicism. Young Americans are flooding into Eastern Orthodox churches.
paragraphs.zh: 有时候我觉得整个世界都变成了那样——在时间中倒退。今天这个世俗化时代里蓬勃发展的宗教运动，多是传统主义取向，对当代文化的大部分内容持反对态度：不仅是伊朗革命后的什叶派伊斯兰，还有东正教犹太教与保守天主教。越来越多的年轻美国人正在涌入东正教教堂。

paragraphs.en: Many of us thought that the world would get more democratic as it modernized, but for the past quarter century, we have seen a reversion to authoritarian strongmen. Donald Trump, acting like some 16th-century European prince, has made the presidency his own personal fiefdom. Vladimir Putin borrows ideas from reactionary thinkers such as Aleksandr Dugin—an Eastern Orthodox, anti-liberal philosopher who rejects the Enlightenment—to justify his imperial conquest of Ukraine.
paragraphs.zh: 我们许多人曾以为世界会在现代化中变得更加民主，但在过去的四分之一个世纪里，我们看到的是向专制强人的回归。唐纳德·特朗普像一位16世纪的欧洲君主，把总统职位变成了他个人的封地。弗拉基米尔·普京则借用了反动思想家如亚历山大·杜金等人的观点——这位东正教的反自由主义哲学家否定启蒙运动——来为其对乌克兰的帝国式征服辩护。

paragraphs.en: If you go on social media, you can see photos of tradwives baking cookies for their husband and five kids. The secretary of health and human services and his followers don’t trust those newfangled inventions, vaccines. In 1999, it seemed that world affairs would be dominated by multilateral groups such as the European Union and the World Trade Organization—but now we are back to 19th-century-style great-power rivalries between China and the United States, between Russia and Europe. Trump’s new National Security Strategy has even revived the Monroe Doctrine.
paragraphs.zh: 如果你刷社交媒体，就能看到“传统贤妻”给丈夫和五个孩子烤饼干的照片。卫生与公众服务部长及其追随者不相信那些新奇发明——疫苗。1999 年时，似乎世界事务将由欧盟和世界贸易组织等多边组织主导，但现在我们又回到了19世纪式的大国对抗：中美之间、俄罗 与欧洲之间。特朗普的新《国家安全战略》甚至让“门罗主义”卷土重来。

paragraphs.en: We used to have a clear idea of where modernity was heading—toward greater autonomy and equality, secularism, stronger individual rights, cultural openness, and liberal democracy. Progress was supposed to lead to the expansion of individual choice in sphere after sphere. Science and reason would prosper while superstition and conspiracy-mongering would wither away.
paragraphs.zh: 我们过去曾清楚地以为现代化的方向是走向更大的自主和平等、世俗化、个人权利更强、文化更开放，以及自由民主。进步本应带来一个领域接一个领域的个人选择扩展。科学和理性会蓬勃发展，而迷信与阴谋论则会逐渐凋零。

paragraphs.en: Turns out that was yesterday’s vision of the future. Billions of people around the world looked at where history was heading and yelled: Stop! They see that future as too spiritually empty, too lonely, too technological, too polluted, too confusing, too incoherent. Whatever their specific complaint, they are driven by a sense of loss, a desire to go back to a simpler, happier, and more sustainable time. Part of the brilliance of the phrase Make America Great Again is that it taps into that sense of nostalgia and loss.
paragraphs.zh: 结果证明，那其实是昨天式的未来想象。世界各地数十亿人看着历史走向，齐声喊道：停下！他们觉得那种未来过于空虚、过于孤立、过于技术化、过于污染、过于混乱、过于支离破碎。无论抱怨的具体内容是什么，他们都带着一种失落感，渴望回到一个更简单、更幸福、更可持续的时代。政治口号“让美国再次伟大”的魅力之一，就在于它正中这一怀旧与失落的情绪。

paragraphs.en: Periods of great disruption inevitably produce yearning for an earlier golden age, and ours is no different. You can tell what kind of reactionary a person is by asking them what era they want to go back to. For some MAGA dudes, it’s the Roman empire, when men were men. For some theocrats, it’s the Middle Ages, when men were monks. In the U.S., many on the right want to go back to the social mores of the 1950s: men in the workplace, women at home; white people on top; epic levels of church attendance; and wholesome fare such as Oklahoma! and Leave It to Beaver onstage and on television. Meanwhile, many on the left want to go back to that decade’s union- and manufacturing-led economy, or to the utopian socialism of the 19th century. Our politics is drenched in nostalgia.
paragraphs.zh: 任何重大变动的时代都不可避免地产生对更早“黄金时代”的向往，我们的时代也不例外。你可以通过问一个人想回到哪个年代来判断他是怎样的反动派。对一些“美国再次伟大”支持者来说，那是“男性即男子汉”的罗马帝国；对一些神权主义者而言，那是“男子皆修道士”的中世纪。在美国，右翼许多人想回到20世纪50年代的社会风气：男人在职场，女人在家；白人居于主导；教堂礼拜名额空前；以及像《俄克拉何马！》《留给比佛》这类“正统”节目在舞台与电视上出现。与此同时，左翼中的许多人想回到那个年代以工会和制造业为核心的经济，或回到19世纪的空想社会主义。我们的政治充满了怀旧情绪。

paragraphs.en: Those of us who believe in progress and the values of the Enlightenment tend to be condescending toward these reactionary impulses. We assume that the reactionaries are unsophisticated, intransigent, parochial—afraid of the freedom that modernity brings. It’s futile to think you can turn back the clock, we say.
paragraphs.zh: 我们这些相信进步并认同启蒙价值观的人，往往对这些反动倾向抱有居高临下的态度。我们会认为反动者既不够成熟，又顽固、狭隘——他们害怕现代性带来的自由。我们说，试图把时钟拨回去是徒劳的。

paragraphs.en: But civilizations turn back the clock all the time. The Italian Renaissance can be seen as a concerted artistic and intellectual effort to return to classical Greek and Roman times. In Lost Enlightenment, the historian S. Frederick Starr recounts how, during the Middle Ages, Central Asia went from being the most scientifically and economically advanced region of the globe to falling behind Europe. During the Ming dynasty, China stopped exploration and de-emphasized scientific progress.
paragraphs.zh: 但文明体总是在把时钟拨回。意大利文艺复兴可以被视为一种有意识的艺术与思想运动，旨在回归古希腊和古罗马时期。在《失落的启蒙》中，历史学家 S. Frederick Starr 回顾了中世纪时期，中亚如何从曾是全球最具科学和经济先进性的地区，跌落到落后于欧洲。又如明代的中国，停止了对外探索，并把科学进步置于次要位置。

paragraphs.en: The 18th-century French Enlightenment cult of reason produced the 19th-century Romantic cult of passion as a counterreaction. The 19th-century explosion of industrialization produced the neo-Gothic reaction, led by people such as John Ruskin, who celebrated pre-machine living.
paragraphs.zh: 18 世纪法国的启蒙理性崇拜，在 19 世纪催生了对立的浪漫主义激情崇拜。19 世纪工业化的爆发又引发了新哥特式反动，由约翰·拉斯金（John Ruskin）等人领导，他们歌颂机器之前的生活方式。

paragraphs.en: For much of the 20th century, faith in progress was the guiding ideology of modernity. Think of all those world’s fairs and theme parks, the giddiness about the wonders of Tomorrowland. That faith in progress was not only a technological one—flying cars!—but a spiritual and moral one. Many, including me, derived meaning from the belief that we were contributing to social progress.
paragraphs.zh: 在20世纪的大部分时间里，对进步的信念一直是现代性的主导意识形态。想想那些世界博览会和主题公园，以及当时人们对“明日乐园”奇迹般景象的狂热。那种进步信念并不仅仅是技术层面的——比如会飞的汽车——而且还是一种精神和道德层面的信念。包括我在内，许多人都在这种信念中找到人生意义：相信自己在推动社会进步。

paragraphs.en: Today, however, billions of people have lost that faith in progress as a source of meaning and are flocking to its opposite. In the 21st century, traditionalism has emerged as a catalytic school of thought. Reactionaries are propelling events, shifting culture and history in their direction. If we want to understand where all of this is taking us, we need to understand what’s driving them and where they get their beliefs. And to contend successfully with the traditionalists’ effects on our politics and culture, we also need to recognize that elements of their worldview are correct. But which parts are correct, and which are completely off the rails?
paragraphs.zh: 然而，如今数十亿人已经不再把进步视为一种意义来源，这一信念正在消退，并转而涌向它的对立面。在21世纪，传统主义成为一种催化性的思想流派。反动派正在推动事件发展，将文化和历史引向他们的方向。如果我们想明白这一切把我们带向何处，就必须弄清是什么在驱动他们，以及他们的观念来源。要想有效应对传统主义者对我们政治与文化的影响，我们还必须承认，他们的世界观中有些部分是正确的。问题在于：哪些是正确的，哪些又完全跑偏了？

paragraphs.en: From the January/February 2023 issue: Derek Thompson on why the age of American progress ended
paragraphs.zh: 摘自2023年1月/2月期：德里克·汤普森（Derek Thompson）关于为何美国“进步时代”已结束

paragraphs.en: If you go spelunking into the mind of a traditionalist of a more intellectual sort, you will usually find Oswald Spengler somewhere deep inside. The first volume of Spengler’s The Decline of the West was published in 1918, just as the First World War was winding down. He argued that each culture has its own unique soul, comprising its habits, customs, and myths. Like any living organism, each culture grows, matures, ages, and dies. In their youthful phases, they display great creativity, a flowering of the arts, an effusion of strong personalities. As they transition to maturity and eventually senescence, they urbanize and bureaucratize, elites lose moral authority, and creativity withers.
paragraphs.zh: 如果你深入一位更具知识分子气质的传统主义者内心世界，你通常会发现在他心中某处都藏着奥斯瓦尔德·施潘格勒（Oswald Spengler）。施潘格勒的《西方的衰落》第一卷于1918年出版，恰逢第一次世界大战即将结束之际。他认为，每一种文化都拥有自己独特的“灵魂”，由习惯、风俗和神话构成。像任何生物一样，每一种文化都会生长、成熟、老去并最终死亡。在其青年阶段，它们展现出巨大的创造力、艺术的盛放、强烈个性的迸发；而当它们进入成熟并最终走向衰老时，便会走向城市化和官僚化，精英失去道德权威，创造力则日益萎缩。

paragraphs.en: Spengler argued that Western culture emerged around the end of the tenth century. He called it “Faustian.” It was individualistic, expansionary, acquisitive, insatiable in its striving. Once a culture slips into its decline phase, it becomes imperialistic and materialistic, and technologists drive what happens. The political system slides into what Spengler called “Caesarism”—rule by despots. Urbanization and industrial growth create masses of atomized people susceptible to demagoguery. Financial power is concentrated in impersonal institutions, weakening the old elites. Large-scale bureaucracy leads to a centralization of power. When crises hit, people want decisive authority. If you believe that our society is in decline, Spengler’s sweeping theories describe and explain what is happening today.
paragraphs.zh: 施潘格勒认为，西方文化大约在10世纪末兴起。他称之为“法斯特式文化”。它以个人主义、扩张性、占有欲和不懈进取为特征。一旦一种文化滑入衰落阶段，就会变得帝国化和唯物主义，并由技术精英主导发展方向。政治体制会滑向施潘格勒所谓的“凯撒主义”——即独裁者统治。城市化和工业化孕育出大量被原子化的人群，容易受到煽动者的操控。金融权力集中于冷冰冰的机构，削弱了旧有精英。大规模的官僚体系导致权力进一步集中。当危机来临时，人们往往渴望果断的权威。如果你相信我们的社会正处于衰落之中，那么施潘格勒那套宏大理论恰好能解释我们今天正在发生的事情。

paragraphs.en: If Spengler was part of the cultural-determinist wing of the interwar reactionary movement, René Guénon was part of the mystical wing. Both men believed the West was in decline, but for different reasons. While the historian Mark Sedgwick was researching his superb book Traditionalism, Dugin told him that what Karl Marx is to communism, Guénon is to traditionalism. Guénon was born in central France in 1886. Throughout his life, he studied various forms of spiritual knowledge—gnosticism, Islam, Taoism, Hinduism. He was not a political writer but a metaphysical one who believed that different religions are living links to the same underlying cosmic truth. He also believed that Western civilization had turned away from this spiritual truth and was living through what Hindu thinkers call the Kali Yuga, the age of corruption and moral decline.
paragraphs.zh: 如果说斯宾格勒属于战间期反动主义运动中强调文化决定论的一支，那么勒内·盖农（René Guénon）则属于其中的神秘主义一支。两个人都认为西方正在衰落，但原因不同。历史学家马克·塞奇威克（Mark Sedgwick）在研究其杰作《传统主义》时，杜京对他说：卡尔·马克思之于共产主义，如同盖农之于传统主义。盖农于1886年出生于法国中部。他一生研究过多种精神知识形态——诺斯替主义、伊斯兰教、道教、印度教。他并非政治作家，而是形而上学作家，认为不同宗教是通向同一宇宙性真理的活生生的纽带。他还认为，西方文明已经背离了这种精神真理，并正在经历印度思想家所称的“劫期”（Kali Yuga）——一个充满腐败和道德衰败的时代。

paragraphs.en: Reading traditionalist writers, you find that each comes up with a different term for the spiritual deadness they associate with modern civilization. Spengler used the word Kulturverfall. For Guénon, that word was quantity. In his 1945 book, The Reign of Quantity and the Signs of the Times, he argued that in this phase of “progressive ‘materialization,’ ” only things that can be counted are considered real.
paragraphs.zh: 阅读传统主义作家时，你会发现每个人都用不同的术语来描述他们认为与现代文明相联系的精神枯竭。斯宾格勒使用了“Kulturverfall”这个词。对盖农而言，那个词则是“量”。在他1945年的著作《数量的统治与时代的征兆》中，他提出，在这种“不断‘物质化’”的阶段，只有能够被计数的事物才被认为是真实的。

paragraphs.en: In the modern age, Guénon continued, science dominates. Modern scientists think they are taking a cold, objective look at reality, but they are pitifully naive, stuck at the level of what scientific materialism and measurement will allow. A modern scientist, in the Guénon view, is oblivious to spiritual reality—which, to the traditionalist, is the primary reality—and so adopts a worldview that denies the existence of the metaphysical realm. The modern scientist is like someone who investigates the workings of an orchestra without the ability to hear music or even the awareness that music exists. All he can describe is bows scraping against strings and air flowing through wind instruments. His theories make a hash of what he is observing, leaving his readers in a flat, soulless realm of disjointed facts.
paragraphs.zh: 盖农继续说，在现代社会，科学占据主导地位。现代科学家认为自己正在以冷静、客观的方式观察现实，但他们可怜地天真，局限于科学唯物主义和可测量性所允许的层面。盖农认为，现代科学家对精神现实视而不见——对传统主义者来说，这是最根本的现实——因此会采取一种否认形而上领域存在的世界观。现代科学家就像一个研究管弦乐团运作的人，却既听不见音乐，也没有意识到音乐这回事。他只能描述弓弦摩擦和气流穿过管乐器所发出的声音。他的理论把正在观察到的对象讲得一塌糊涂，将读者置于一个平板、无灵魂、支离破碎的事实世界里。

paragraphs.en: From the February 2025 issue: Anne Applebaum on RFK Jr. and the end of Enlightenment rationality
paragraphs.zh: 摘自2025年2月刊：安妮·阿普尔鲍姆（Anne Applebaum）关于罗伯特·F·肯尼迪二世（RFK Jr.）与启蒙理性主义终结的观点

paragraphs.en: The modern person senses a vacuum where his spiritual life should be. He covers the hole in his soul with ceaseless agitation, unending change, and ever-increasing speed. “The dominant impression today,” Guénon wrote more than 80 years ago, is “an impression of instability extending to all domains.” His commitment to traditionalist spirituality brought him eventually to Sufism, a mystical strain of Islam. He converted, moved to Cairo, married an Egyptian woman, and died there in 1951.
paragraphs.zh: 现代人会感觉到精神生活本应栖息的地方出现了空洞。他用无休止的骚动、连续不断的变化和越来越快的节奏去填补灵魂的这处裂缝。古冉（Guénon）在八十多年前写道，如今“给人的主要印象是：一种延伸到各个领域的不稳定感。”他对传统灵性道路的坚守最终将他带向苏菲主义这一伊斯兰神秘主义传统。他皈依了伊斯兰，移居开罗，娶了埃及妻子，并于1951年在那里的生命中走到了尽头。

paragraphs.en: Guénon had a profound influence on Julius Evola, an Italian writer who had a brief moment of celebrity in the American press in 2017, after the media revealed that the Trump adviser Steve Bannon had referenced Evola during a conference at the Vatican. The white nationalist Richard Spencer called Evola “one of the most fascinating men of the 20th century.”
paragraphs.zh: 古冉对朱利叶斯·埃沃拉（Julius Evola）影响深远。2017年，埃沃拉短暂成为美国媒体的名人，因为有报道称美国总统顾问史蒂夫·班农（Steve Bannon）在梵蒂冈的一次会议上引用过他。白人至上主义者理查德·斯宾塞（Richard Spencer）称埃沃拉是“20世纪最迷人的人物之一”。

paragraphs.en: Evola was born in Rome, fought as an artillery officer in World War I, and then became an artist in the Dada movement. He agreed with Guénon that we are living in an age of corruption that has turned its back on spiritual truth. In 1934, he published a manifesto called Revolt Against the Modern World. Evola broke with Guénon, however, by embracing politics after World War II and becoming the chief ideologue of the Italian far right. His views were anti-egalitarian, anti-liberal, antidemocratic. He was pro-monarchy and pro-hierarchy, and supported a racial caste system. He was post-liberal before being post-liberal was cool.
paragraphs.zh: 埃沃拉出生于罗马，第一次世界大战时曾是一名炮兵军官，之后成为达达主义（Dada）运动中的一位艺术家。他同意古冉的观点：我们正生活在一个背弃灵性真理、充满腐败的时代。1934年，他出版了一本宣言《反对现代世界》。然而，二战后，埃沃拉转向政治，并成为意大利极右翼的主要意识形态理论家，从而与古冉决裂。他的观点反平等、反自由主义、反民主；他支持君主制和等级秩序，并主张种族种姓体系。他甚至可以说是“后自由主义”先驱，即在后自由主义被普遍认同以前就提出了这种立场。

paragraphs.en: Benito Mussolini was a big fan, but Evola criticized fascism for accepting too much of the modern world. What is necessary, Evola argued, is a “race of masters,” who will lead a “revolt from the depths.” Any attempt to make a better world with spiritually stunted people will fail—because spiritually stunted people chase shallow, hedonistic values, and a noble society can be built only by those whose lives are oriented toward spiritual excellence.
paragraphs.zh: 贝尼托·墨索里尼（Benito Mussolini）是他的忠实支持者，但埃沃拉批评法西斯主义过度接受了现代世界。他认为，必要的是一群“主人阶级”（race of masters），他们将发起一场“自深层发起的反叛”。任何试图用精神发展受限的人来建造更美好世界的努力都将失败，因为精神上迟钝的人只会追逐浅薄而享乐主义的价值观；只有那些把人生方向指向精神卓越的人，才能建立起真正高尚的社会。

paragraphs.en: Evola was political where Guénon was not, bluntly racist where Guénon was not. Gábor Vona, a prominent hard-right politician in Hungary, called Evola “one of the greatest thinkers of the twentieth century” in the foreword to a 2012 selection of Evola’s writing titled A Handbook for Right-Wing Youth. Today many of us look at Europe’s far-right parties and see pseudo–storm troopers. But those parties see themselves as a spiritual vanguard trying to preserve the highest registers of the soul.
paragraphs.zh: 埃沃拉（Evola）涉足政治，而吉农（Guénon）并未；埃沃拉公开种族主义，而吉农则不是。2012年，匈牙利一位知名的极右翼政客加博尔·沃纳（Gábor Vona）在《右翼青年手册》（A Handbook for Right-Wing Youth）这一伊科瓦文集的序言中称其为“20世纪最伟大的思想家之一”。今天，我们很多人看到欧洲的极右翼政党会想起一群“伪冲锋队”式的人物。但在这些政党看来，他们自己是精神上的前沿阵线，试图守护灵魂的最高层级。

paragraphs.en: Understanding contemporary traditionalism requires understanding its intellectual underpinnings in the thinking of these forefathers. All traditionalists tell a story about a time when people were rooted in stable homes and a way of life that got destroyed by a historic rupture that ushered in the soulless modern era—whether they call that era Faustian Civilization (Spengler), the Age of Quantity (Guénon), the Kali Yuga (Guénon and Evola), or something else.
paragraphs.zh: 要理解当代传统主义，就必须理解这些先行者思想中的知识基础。所有传统主义者都在讲述一个关于过去时代的故事：人们曾扎根于稳定的家园与生活方式，但在一次历史性断裂中被摧毁，从而迈入了毫无灵魂的现代时代——无论他们将这一时代称为“浮士德式文明”（Spengler）、“数量时代”（Guénon）、“劫初降世纪”（Kali Yuga，出自Guénon与Evola）还是其他什么名称。

paragraphs.en: Today’s traditionalists do not agree on when history took a wrong turn. But they all tell some sort of decline story. “It’s an empirical fact that basically everything in our day to day lives has gotten worse over the years,” the right-wing podcaster Matt Walsh has written. “The quality of everything—food, clothing, entertainment, air travel, roads, traffic, infrastructure, housing, etc—has declined in observable ways.”
paragraphs.zh: 当代传统主义者并不统一于历史何时偏离正轨；但他们都在叙述某种衰败的故事。右翼播客人马特·沃什（Matt Walsh）写道：“有一个实证事实：我们日常生活中的几乎一切，随着时间推移都在变得更糟。” “无论是食品、服装、娱乐、空中旅行、道路、交通、基础设施、住房等一切，质量都以可观察的方式下降了。”

paragraphs.en: R. R. Reno is the editor of First Things, one of the most influential Catholic traditionalist magazines in America. Reno’s story of decline doesn’t really start until just after World War II. During the first half of the 20th century, he argues, Westerners lived amid rivers of blood—wars, revolutions, genocides. After the Nazis were defeated, many people across the West concluded that the savagery had been unleashed by strong attachments to nations, ideologies, homeland, race. To head off future world wars, people across a range of sectors felt it necessary to create a culture that would prevent the strong beliefs and loyalties that might lead to fanaticism and war. A representative example is the philosopher Karl Popper, a champion of the scientific method, who wrote The Open Society and Its Enemies, which celebrates minds and nations that are not closed around core truths but are instead perpetually open to new possibilities. Forms of critical thinking were elevated to undermine grand philosophies. Moral relativism—the idea that it’s up to each person to find their own values and truth—prevailed. Children were raised in permissive environments to foster greater pluralism. As Reno puts it in Return of the Strong Gods, postwar thinkers came to a fundamental conclusion: “Whatever is strong—strong loves and strong truths—leads to oppression, while liberty and prosperity require the reign of weak loves and weak truths.”
paragraphs.zh: R. R.雷诺（R. R. Reno）是《第一杂志》（First Things）的主编，这本刊物是美国最具影响力的天主教传统主义杂志之一。雷诺的衰败叙事并不是从更早时期开始的，而是几乎始于二战结束后。他认为，在20世纪上半叶，西方人生活在鲜血河流之中——战争、革命、种族灭绝。纳粹战败后，西方许多人得出结论，正是对国家、意识形态、祖国、种族这些强烈依附感释放了残暴。为了防止未来的世界大战，人们跨越不同领域后感到有必要创造一种文化，以避免强烈信念和忠诚感将人们推向狂热主义和战争。一个代表性例子是哲学家卡尔·波普尔（Karl Popper），他是科学方法的倡导者，写下了《开放社会及其敌人》，在书中称赞那些不围绕“核心真理”自我封闭、而是始终向新可能性敞开的心灵与民族。所谓“批判性思维”被提升到一种高度，旨在削弱宏大哲学。道德相对主义——即真理与价值由每个人自行寻找——于是盛行。儿童在宽松环境中被抚育，以培育更高程度的多元主义。正如雷诺在《强者之神归来》（Return of the Strong Gods）中所说，战后思想家得出一个根本结论： “任何强大的东西——强烈的爱与强烈的真理——都会导致压迫；只有软弱的爱与软弱的真理，才可能孕育自由与繁荣。”

paragraphs.en: This cult of openness, Reno observes, was bipartisan. Liberals believed in lifestyle freedom and conservatives believed in economic freedom, but they both believed in the primacy of individual choice. You do you; I’ll do me.
paragraphs.zh: Reno 指出，这种开放主义的信条本是跨越党派的。自由派信奉生活方式自由，保守派信奉经济自由，但两者都相信个人选择的优先性。你由着你，我按我的来。

paragraphs.en: But all of this openness didn’t lead to a nirvana of free individuals. It led, the traditionalists believe, to a society in which social bonds were attenuated. It led to a nihilistic society in which people could find no grand purpose. It led to a consumerist society in which people shopped to fill their spiritual void. It led, in Reno’s words, to “dissolution, disintegration, and deconsolidation.”
paragraphs.zh: 但这一切开放并没有导向自由个体的乐园。正如传统主义者所认为的，它导向了一个社会关系被削弱的社会。它导向了一个虚无主义社会，在那里人们找不到宏大的共同目标。它导向了一个消费主义社会，在那里人们通过购物来填补内心的精神真空。正如Reno所言，它导致了“解体、分解和离散”。

paragraphs.en: “Unable to identify our shared loves,” Reno writes, “we cannot identify the common good, the res in the res publica.” Civic life collapses.
paragraphs.zh: “当我们无法辨认共同的所爱时，”Reno 写道，“我们也就无法辨认公共善，即共和共同体中的共同行善之基石。” 公民生活由此崩塌。

paragraphs.en: Though today traditionalism mostly lives on the right, it sometimes emanates from the far left. The British writer Paul Kingsnorth, for instance, was a radical-left environmentalist before becoming an Orthodox Christian traditionalist. The two positions are not so different—both reject technocratic modernity. Kingsnorth has his own term for the spiritual deadness of modern life: “the Machine,” which, in his telling, comprises all of capitalist, technocratic society. In his book Against the Machine, he describes a visit to a grocery store: “I saw the sheer unnaturalness of this way of obtaining food, and the unnaturalness, too, of our wandering these straight lined, strip-lit plastic aisles inside this giant metal box instead of gathering mushrooms from a forest floor.” Kingsnorth’s writing has a strong Small Is Beautiful hippie vibe, but he goes beyond that. “The degree of control and monitoring which we endure in ‘developed’ societies, which has been accelerating for decades and which has reached warp speed in the 2020s, is creating a kind of digital holding camp in which we all find ourselves trapped.” He sees the ideology of the Machine as a “liberation of individual desire” that effaces our communal civilizational bonds and turns our world into “a blank slate to be written on afresh when the old limits of nature and culture are washed away. This is our faith: that breaking boundaries leads to happiness.”
paragraphs.zh: 尽管当下传统主义主要出现在右翼，它有时也会源于极左阵营。例如，英国作家保罗·金斯诺思（Paul Kingsnorth）曾是激进左翼环保主义者，后来成为一名东正教传统主义者。这两个立场并不那么不同——都反对技术官僚式的现代性。金斯诺思为现代生活的精神枯竭有自己的称呼：他称之为“机器”（Machine），在他看来，它包括了全部资本主义的技术官僚社会。在他的《反对机器》一书里，他描述了一次去杂货店的经历：“我看到了以这种方式获取食物的绝对不自然，也看到了我们在这座巨大金属箱里，在那些笔直、条状、被塑料条照亮的过道里徘徊的那种不自然，而不是在森林地面采蘑菇。” 金斯诺思的文字带有浓重的《小而美》（Small Is Beautiful）式嬉皮气质，但他并未止步于此。“在我们这些‘发达’社会里，我们所承受的控制与监控程度，几十年来一直在加速，到2020年代更是到了超光速，这正在制造一种数字化的拘留营，我们都被困在其中。” 他把机器的意识形态看作一种“个体欲望解放”，它抹消了我们文明中的共同纽带，并把世界变成“当自然和文化的旧边界被冲刷走后可重新书写的一块白板。这就是我们的信念：破除边界就会带来幸福。”

paragraphs.en: The Machine is not only a system outside of us but a state of mind within us, one built around rationalism, economics, scientism, optimization, and efficiency. Its impulse is to use pure reason to achieve power, control, and domination. We like to think the Nazis were fanatics who operated outside of reason. Not so, Kingsnorth argues. They were consummate embodiments of the rationalist project, using social science to engineer what they took to be the optimal society.
paragraphs.zh: “机器”不仅是我们外在的一个系统，也是我们内在的一种心态，其核心围绕理性主义、经济学、科学主义、优化和效率。它的冲动是用纯粹理性去追求权力、控制与支配。我们喜欢认为纳粹是脱离理性、疯狂到近乎狂热的极端分子。可不是这样，金斯诺思指出，恰恰相反，他们是理性主义工程计划的典范体现，运用社会科学来“设计”他们认为最优化的社会。

paragraphs.en: The rationalist Machine seeks to merge your mind with the AI bots that are turning you into something less than fully human. Kingsnorth quotes a famous line from Wendell Berry: “The next great division of the world will be between people who wish to live as creatures and people who wish to live as machines.”
paragraphs.zh: 这套理性化的“机器”试图把你的大脑与正在把你变成不再完整人的 AI 机器人融合在一起。金斯诺思引用了温德尔·贝里的名言：“世界的下一个巨大分野，将在那些希望像生物一样生活的人与那些希望像机器一样生活的人之间产生。”

paragraphs.en: What do the traditionalists offer as a replacement for contemporary culture?
paragraphs.zh: 传统主义者能为当代文化提供什么样的替代？

paragraphs.en: First, they offer roots. The master trend of modernity is freedom. You get to do what you want. You can go to college far away, move from city to city, surf through different cultures and lifestyle options.
paragraphs.zh: 首先，他们给出的，是“根”。现代性的主流趋势是自由。你可以做你想做的事。你可以去很远的地方上大学，搬来搬去，在不同城市间生活，也可以在各种文化和生活方式之间切换。

paragraphs.en: This, traditionalists charge, leads to an aimless, ephemeral life. “The modern person belongs everywhere and nowhere at once,” Alan Noble, a literature professor at Oklahoma Baptist University, writes in You Are Not Your Own: Belonging to God in an Inhuman World. Such a person is perpetually sampling experiences but is not rooted. When the so-called abundance progressives argue that America has a housing crisis, the traditionalists counter that what America really has is a home crisis. Cultural change and mass immigration mean that people can’t even feel at home in their own country.
paragraphs.zh: 传统主义者指责这一点，会导致一种毫无方向、虚无缥缈的生活。俄克拉荷马浸信会大学文学教授艾伦·诺布尔在《你不是你自己：在非人类世界中归属上帝》一书中写道：“现代人既属于每个地方，又同时不属于任何地方。”这样的人不断试探各种体验，却没有根基。所谓“富足进步主义者”主张美国有一场住房危机时，传统主义者反驳说，美国真正面对的其实是“家园危机”。文化变迁和大规模移民意味着人们甚至不能在自己国家感到“在家”。

paragraphs.en: Traditionalists, by contrast, offer stable attachments. For the traditionalist, the primary unit of modern social life is not the sovereign, free-choosing individual; it is the social covenant that connects people. We are not born into a void. We are born into particular families, particular neighborhoods, particular tribes, particular faiths. Your life is connected via a great chain of bonds to your ancestors, whom you honor, and to future generations, whom you serve. In the traditionalist imagining, people are planted in the spot of earth where the bones of their ancestors lie, the place where they can be intimately known and deeply loved, where stories and skills get passed down by elders, and where they know the hills and trees so well that their contours are carved into the heart. Living up to your covenantal obligations constitutes the essence of moral life. Traditionalists are willing to accept limits on their freedom if it enables them to live within a local network of strong attachments that give life meaning.
paragraphs.zh: 相比之下，传统主义者提供的是稳定的归属。对传统主义者而言，现代社会生活的基本单位不是主权独立、自由选择的个体，而是连接人的社会共同体。我们不是在真空中诞生，而是出生在特定的家庭、特定的社区、特定的部落、特定的信仰之中。你的生活通过一条很长的纽带链与祖先相连，你要尊敬他们；也与后代相连，你要服务于他们。在传统主义的设想里，人被安置在祖先骨骼长眠的大地之上，在那里他们可以被亲密地认识和深情地爱护，老一辈传下故事与技艺，你对山丘和树木熟悉到它们的轮廓都刻进内心。履行盟约式的责任构成了道德生活的核心。传统主义者愿意接受对自由的限制，只要这能让他们生活在一个由稳固牵连构成的地方网络中，并从中获得生命的意义。

paragraphs.en: Second, traditionalists offer enchantment. Moderns, they believe, live within what Max Weber called the “iron cage” of rationalism and bureaucracy, which is denuded of any enchantment. The goals of science and capitalism are pragmatic, materialistic, and instrumental. In a disenchanted world, religion withers, and so do the humanities, the poetic, and the spiritual. To borrow from R. R. Reno, why read Middlemarch when you can learn about marriage from a behavioral economist armed with studies, correlations, and standard deviations?
paragraphs.zh: 其次，传统主义者提供了“魅惑”或“神圣感”。他们认为，现代人生活在马克斯·韦伯所说的“理性主义与官僚主义的铁笼”里，在这个世界里魅力和神圣感被剥离殆尽。科学与资本主义的目标是实用、唯物和工具化的。在一个被去魅的世界中，宗教萎缩，连人文学科、诗意和灵性也随之消退。借用R. R.雷诺的话说，既然有行为经济学家拿着研究、相关性和标准差告诉你婚姻，该还为什么要读《米德尔马契》呢？

paragraphs.en: Traditionalists generally believe in a transcendent realm of the spirit that exists above and prior to the world we experience through our senses. This transcendent level of reality is independent of you—it is ordained by God, contained within the mysteries of nature, expressed through myth and song more than through analytical thinking. “Every culture, whether it knows it or not, is built around a sacred order,” Kingsnorth writes. “This does not, of course, need to be a Christian order. It could be Islamic, Hindu or Daoist. It could be based on the veneration of ancestors or the worship of Odin. But there is a throne at the heart of every culture, and whoever sits on it will be the force you take your instruction from.”
paragraphs.zh: 传统主义者普遍相信存在一个超越的精神领域，位于我们通过感官经验的世界之上并先于它。这个超越的现实层面不依赖于个人，它由上帝所定，包含在自然的奥秘之中，通过神话和歌谣比通过分析思维更充分地被表达出来。“每一种文化，不论自觉与否，都建立在一种神圣秩序之上，”金斯诺斯写道。“当然，这不必然是基督教秩序，也可以是伊斯兰、印度教或道教。它也许以祖先崇敬为核心，也可以以奥丁崇拜为核心。但每种文化的核心都有一把王座，而坐在王座上的力量，就是你所听命遵循的权威。”

paragraphs.en: Third, traditionalists offer moral order. Good and evil are not matters of personal choice. Natural law is woven by God into the fabric of the universe. Traditionalists get worked up when they find themselves in a culture that can no longer define what a woman is, because they believe that categories like gender are elemental to natural law.
paragraphs.zh: 第三，传统主义者提供道德秩序。善与恶不是个人选择的问题。自然法则被上帝编织进宇宙的结构之中。传统主义者会对自己身处的文化感到愤怒：这种文化已无法再定义“女人”到底是什么，因为他们相信，性别等范畴是自然法则中最基本的要素。

paragraphs.en: The fourth thing that traditionalists offer their flock is protection against the cultural depredations of modernity. Modern progressives decry the evils of colonialism. But to the traditionalist, progressives are themselves colonialists: Their educators determine what ideologies will be pumped into your kid’s brain, their psychologists redefine how you should raise your family, their thought police determine what words can come out of your mouth. To the traditionalist, professional experts—social workers, university administrators, therapists, DEI officers, and the media—are the storm troopers of elite domination. In response to all of this, traditionalists seek to help people recapture control of their own culture.
paragraphs.zh: 传统主义者为其追随者提供的第四件事是对现代性文化掠夺的防护。现代进步派会谴责殖民主义的罪恶。但在传统主义者看来，进步派本人就是殖民者：他们的教育者决定要向你的孩子大脑里灌输什么意识形态，他们的心理学家重新定义你该如何养育家庭，他们的“思想警察”决定你嘴里能说哪些词。对传统主义者而言，专业精英——社会工作者、大学行政人员、治疗师、DEI官员和媒体——都是精英统治的冲锋队。面对这一切，传统主义者试图帮助人们夺回对自身文化的控制。

paragraphs.en: The people I’m quoting in this essay are mostly intellectuals, but their loyalty is with the working class because they share (or at least think they share) the same beliefs. “Lower-middle-class culture, now as in the past, is organized around family, church, and neighborhood,” the historian Christopher Lasch wrote in The True and Only Heaven. “It values the community’s continuity more highly than individual advancement, solidarity more highly than social mobility.” The working class doesn’t require seminars to teach it traditionalism; it grasps the concept intuitively.
paragraphs.zh: 我在这篇文章中引用的人大多是知识分子，但他们对工人阶级忠诚，因为他们共享（或者至少认为自己共享）同样的信念。历史学家克里斯托弗·拉什在《The True and Only Heaven》中写道：“如今如以往，中下层阶级文化围绕家庭、教会和社区组织。”他还写道：“它更重视社区的延续性，而非个人的进步，更重视团结，而非社会流动。”工人阶级不需要研讨班来教他们传统主义；他们凭直觉就能把这个概念抓住。

paragraphs.en: The culture war between the modernists and the traditionalists is not just between classes within nations, but between civilizations. Every few years, the World Values Survey studies various cultures around the globe. Protestant Europe and the English-speaking world, including the United States, stand out for their tremendous emphasis on individual autonomy, self-expression, and secular social values. Most of the rest of the world places higher value on traditional family arrangements, the importance of religion, and respect for authority. We moderns may think we own the future, but the traditionalists like their chances. If this is a global culture war, it’s the whole world against us.
paragraphs.zh: 现代主义者与传统主义者之间的文化战争，不仅是国家内部阶层之间的冲突，更是文明之间的冲突。世界价值观调查每隔几年就会研究全球各地的不同文化。新教欧洲和英语世界（包括美国）在个人自主、个体表达和世俗社会价值观方面表现出极强的重视。世界上大多数其他地区则更看重传统家庭结构、宗教的重要性以及对权威的尊重。我们这些“现代人”也许以为我们掌握了未来，但传统主义者相信他们占据优势。若这是场全球性的文化战争，那么这是全世界都站在我们对立面。

paragraphs.en: The reason I’ve dwelled at such length on the tenets and features of traditionalism is that I want my description to be accurate enough that traditionalists will see themselves in it, and to be detailed enough that even progressive, Enlightenment-loving moderns might understand the appeal of traditionalist ideas.
paragraphs.zh: 我之所以如此详细地阐述传统主义的信条和特征，是因为我希望我的描述足够准确，让传统主义者能够在其中认出自己；也希望足够细致，以至于即使是崇尚进步和启蒙精神的现代人，也能理解传统主义思想的吸引力。

paragraphs.en: I confess that I feel a modicum of sympathy for some of the traditionalist arguments. One of my favorite insights from psychology is that a successful, well-adjusted life consists of daring explorations from a secure base. The traditionalists are right to say that one of the central problems in America and the West today is that many people have lost that secure base—a stable home and community, solid emotional connections, financial security, a coherent culture, and an understanding that our lives are contained within a shared moral order.
paragraphs.zh: 我承认我对一些传统主义论点抱有一丝同情。心理学里我最喜欢的一条洞见是：一段成功而身心健康的人生，离不开从一个可靠基础出发的大胆探索。传统主义者说得对，今天美国和西方面临的核心问题之一是，许多人已经失去了这种可靠基础——稳定的家庭与社区、牢固的情感联结、经济安全、连贯的文化，以及我们生活于共同道德秩序之中的这一认知。

paragraphs.en: My problem with the traditionalists is that I don’t agree with them about what a flourishing life looks like. Traditionalists strike me as the kind of people who would score extremely low on the personality trait called “openness to experience.” They focus overwhelmingly on the secure base and seem to have no interest in daring adventures. They seem to want to lead stationary lives.
paragraphs.zh: 我不同意传统主义者的地方在于，我不认同他们对“繁荣人生”应是什么样子的定义。传统主义者在我看来，像是那些在“经验开放性”这种人格特质上会被评分极低的人。他们几乎完全只盯着“可靠基础”，似乎对冒险探索不感兴趣。看起来他们想要的是一种安于现状的生活。

paragraphs.en: That’s fine. Different strokes for different folks. But the traditionalists distort history when they write it as if all people have always wanted stationary lives and our goal as a society should be to make stationary lives the norm.
paragraphs.zh: 这一点没问题，人有不同的偏好，这很正常。但传统主义者在叙述历史时出现了歪曲：他们把历史写成仿佛所有人从来都想过安于现状的生活，并且将社会的目标设定为让这种静止的生活成为常态。

paragraphs.en: All traditionalists, from Spengler to Kingsnorth, tell a story about a historic rupture that destroyed the ancestral culture and gave rise to the rootless, soulless modern era. But no such historic rupture ever happened. Nor has there been a moment when humans were forever content to stay within the safety of their village. History has always been lived within the tension between the desire for security and the desire for learning, exploration, movement, and growth. The early hominids of the species Homo erectus may have loved their small African communities 1.9 million years ago—but they still ventured forth to places as far as China and Indonesia. The early Polynesians may have loved their home islands—but they still felt the urge to explore and settle an array of tiny islands in an expanse of ocean spanning millions of square miles. (And they did this in a time without modern navigation devices, when one slight steering error could set you astray in the enormous, empty Pacific.)
paragraphs.zh: 从斯宾格勒到金斯诺思，所有传统主义者都讲述着这样一个故事：历史上曾有一次重大的断裂，摧毁了祖先文化，并催生了一个没有根、没有灵魂的现代时代。但这样的历史断裂从未发生过。人类也从未有过一个时刻是永远满足于待在村落安全范围之内的。历史始终在两种力量之间展开：一方面是对安全的渴望，另一方面是对学习、探索、流动与成长的追求。约190万年前，早期直立人可能热爱非洲的小型社群，但他们仍然走向了远方，前往过中国和印度尼西亚。早期的波利尼西亚人也许热爱自己的故土岛屿——但他们同样受到探索和定居强烈冲动的驱使，陆续在横跨数百万平方英里的大洋上，点亮一片片小岛屿。（而他们是在没有现代导航设备的时代做到这一点的，那时哪怕微小的航向偏差，都可能让人迷失在广阔而空旷的太平洋中。）

paragraphs.en: Human beings have a need for both security and exploration, for both belonging and autonomy, for both stability and innovation. Our lives are propelled by these contradictions, which can never be resolved.
paragraphs.zh: 人类既需要安全，也需要探索；既需要归属，也需要自主；既需要稳定，也需要创新。我们的生活正是被这些彼此矛盾、却又相互推动的力量驱动，而这种矛盾永远无法彻底解决。

paragraphs.en: Traditionalists are trying to live the monist dream—the dream that we can build a society in which all the pieces fit neatly together. But the many and diverse values that humans cherish will never fit neatly together. In every culture, groups argue over which values should have priority in present circumstances. There’s never been a tranquil resting spot, and there never will be.
paragraphs.zh: 传统主义者试图实现一种一元论式的理想：相信我们可以建构一个所有要素都能井然契合的社会。但人类珍视的诸多价值十分多元且彼此差异，它们永远无法被整齐拼合在一起。每个文化中，群体都会围绕在当下情境中哪些价值应当优先发生争论。历史上从未有过平静的避风港，也绝不会有。

paragraphs.en: From the November 2025 issue: David Brooks on how America needs a mass movement—now
paragraphs.zh: 摘自2025年11月刊：戴维·布鲁克斯：美国为何需要一场大众运动——就在当下

paragraphs.en: Some traditionalists talk as if early or medieval Christendom is the static utopia they yearn for. Once upon a time, people lived close to the soil and were enveloped by faith—until those dehumanizing forces of democracy, capitalism, science, and technology ruined everything. But Judaism and Christianity are not separate from democracy, capitalism, science, and the rest of modernity. In fact, they provided many of the rules and ideas that are the basis of post-Enlightenment modernity: all humans are morally equal; respect individual conscience; history moves in a linear direction; every person has their own calling, as well as inalienable rights. Jesus was hardly a supporter of stasis. He was a Jewish radical who turned all the power structures of his society upside down.
paragraphs.zh: 一些传统主义者说起往昔或中世纪的基督教世界时，似乎把它当作他们向往的静态乌托邦。那种叙事认为，人们曾经与土地相连、被信仰包围，直到民主、资本主义、科学和技术这些“去人性化”力量出现后，一切都被毁坏了。可惜的是，犹太教和基督教并非与民主、资本主义、科学及现代性其他组成部分彼此隔绝。事实上，正是它们提供了许多构成后启蒙现代性的规则与观念：所有人都在道德上平等；尊重个人良知；历史按线性方向推进；每个人都有自己的使命与不可剥夺的权利。耶稣绝不是停滞不前的拥护者。他是一个犹太激进主义者，把社会中的所有权力结构都颠倒过来。

paragraphs.en: Let me offer my own historical narrative and talk about where it overlaps with the traditionalist one and where it diverges. The story I tell is a long procession of stumbles. Some eras are more communal and some eras are more individualistic; some are more religious and some are more secular. But in the West, these cultural shifts have mostly been led by people trying to move humanity forward, in response to the needs of the moment. The stumbling process can be ugly—wars, atrocities, communism. But generally we have stumbled forward. At Harvard, the cognitive scientist Steven Pinker spent a decade or so collecting an Everest of data showing that life since the Enlightenment has been getting more peaceful, more affluent, more comfortable, happier, and more learned—as well as simply longer. And our progress is not just material; it’s moral. Things that our society used to tolerate—torture, slavery, cruelty—have been deemed unacceptable by both law and custom. The late political scientist James Q. Wilson wrote in The Moral Sense that “the most remarkable change in the moral history of mankind has been the rise—and occasionally the application—of the view that all people, and not just one’s own kind, are entitled to fair treatment.”
paragraphs.zh: 我想提供我自己的历史叙事，并说明它与传统主义叙事重叠的地方与分歧的地方。我所讲述的故事是一长串跌跌撞撞的前行。一些时代更具共同体特征，一些时代则更强调个人；有些更宗教化，有些更世俗化。但在西方，这些文化转变大多由试图推动人类向前的人引领，他们是在回应当下的需求。这样的跌跌撞撞并不总是优雅——战争、暴行、共产主义都是证据。但总体而言，我们是在蹒跚中前行。在哈佛，认知科学家斯蒂芬·平克曾花了十年左右时间，积累了堆积如山的数据，显示启蒙运动以来的生活变得更和平、更富足、更舒适、更幸福、更博学，也更长寿。我们的进步不仅是物质层面的；它还是道德层面的。曾经社会容忍的事情——酷刑、奴隶制、残忍——如今已被法律与风俗共同视为不可接受。已故政治学家詹姆斯·Q·威尔逊在《道德感》中写道：‘人类道德史上最显著的变化，就是这种观点的兴起——并偶尔付诸实践——即所有人，不仅仅是自己同类，都有资格获得公平对待。’

paragraphs.en: I look across the past 70 years—years the traditionalists say are filled with moral rot—and I see an astounding widening of the circle of concern. Segregation and racism have been reduced. Billions of women have a greater chance to gain power and professional success equal to men’s. Colonialism has been repudiated. We’ve seen the greatest reduction in global poverty in the history of the world. America has expanded opportunity beyond white, Protestant men. We’ve even passed laws to reduce cruelty to animals.
paragraphs.zh: 当我回望过去七十年——传统主义者所说充满道德腐朽的七十年——我看到令人震惊的是社会关怀范围的持续扩大。种族隔离和种族歧视有所减少。数十亿女性拥有了比过去更大的机会，获得与男性相当的权力和职业成功。殖民主义已被公开否定。我们见证了世界历史上规模最大的全球贫困下降。美国把机会扩展到了白人新教男性之外。我们甚至通过了减少对动物残酷行为的法律。

paragraphs.en: But even in the historical story I tell, every moment of great cultural or social advance has had a cost. Over these past 70 years of progress, our culture has moved in the direction of autonomy, individualism, and choice. This has generated creativity and freedom, but it has weakened the bonds between people and the elemental commitments that precede choice—to family, neighborhood, faith, and nation. As part of this general tendency toward individualism, we have privatized morality, telling people to come up with their own values.
paragraphs.zh: 但即便在我讲述的历史中，每一次重大的文化或社会进步都伴随着代价。回看这过去七十年的进步，我们的文化也在朝着自主、个人主义和选择走去。它带来了创造力和自由，却削弱了人们之间的联结，也削弱了那些先于选择而存在的根本承诺——对家庭、社区、信仰和国家的承诺。作为这种普遍走向个人主义的一部分，我们道德观也被私有化，人们被告知要自己来构造价值体系。

paragraphs.en: Freedom is great, but not if you don’t know what ultimate end you are seeking. Our modern, individualistic culture has fallen for the belief that individuals are capable of devising their own morality. No historical evidence supports this belief.
paragraphs.zh: 自由很可贵，但前提是你知道自己追求的最终目标是什么。我们现代的个人主义文化迷信一种观点：个体能够自己编造道德规范。历史上没有任何证据支持这种观点。

paragraphs.en: As we’ve advanced scientifically and technologically, we’ve forgotten something that the traditionalists understand: People absorb their moral values, their sense of purpose, and their way of life from within a tradition. The most important text of the Western moral tradition is the Bible. Even figures who were not great or conventional religious believers—such as Shakespeare, Jefferson, and Lincoln—knew their Bible. The second-most-important tradition of moral wisdom is the body of work we call humanism—the great novels, paintings, poems, dramas, histories, and philosophical tracts by thinkers and artists from all over the globe.
paragraphs.zh: 随着科学和技术的发展，我们却忘记了传统主义者所理解的一点：人们从传统中吸收自己的道德价值、人生意义和生活方式。西方道德传统最重要的文本就是《圣经》。即使是那些并非虔诚信徒、并不算典型宗教信仰者的人，如莎士比亚、杰斐逊和林肯，也都熟悉圣经。次重要的道德智慧传统是我们称之为人文主义的思想体系——来自全球思想家和艺术家创作的杰出小说、绘画、诗歌、戏剧、历史与哲学论文。

paragraphs.en: David Brooks: A humanist manifesto
paragraphs.zh: 大卫·布鲁克斯：《人文主义宣言》

paragraphs.en: In our rush toward autonomy, we have failed to pass down these sources of moral wisdom from one generation to the next. The ethos of individualism has led us to cut ourselves off from our own traditions: We’re so focused on the individual self that we fail to appreciate the millennia-long conversations within which each self swims. This rejection of tradition has been driven partly by ideology. In 1987, a group of progressive students from Stanford chanted “Hey, hey, ho, ho, Western Culture has got to go!” They were part of a multigenerational effort led by people who thought that because Western civilization had produced colonialism, the whole collective wisdom of the Western tradition should be thrown in the trash. But I think the bigger cause was simple shortsightedness. Several generations of parents, educators, and students decided that the most important subjects to study are those that can help you make money. They didn’t recognize the value of the humanities.
paragraphs.zh: 在我们急于走向自主的过程中，我们没有把这些道德智慧的来源一代一代地传承下去。个人主义的精神让我们把自己与传统切断：我们过于专注于个人自我，以致无法体会到每个自我都在其中游弋的跨千年对话。这种否定传统在某种程度上是意识形态推动的。1987年，一群斯坦福的进步主义学生高喊“Hey, hey, ho, ho, Western Culture has got to go（呃，西方文化得走）！”他们是由一些人领导的跨代运动的一部分，这些人认为既然西方文明孕育了殖民主义，就应该把西方传统的全部集体智慧都扔进垃圾桶。但我认为更根本的原因是简单的目光短浅。几代父母、教育者和学生都认为最重要的学科是那些能帮助你挣钱的课程。于是他们未能意识到人文学科的价值。

paragraphs.en: The loss of civilizational and moral knowledge that this has entailed has had practical consequences: sloppy thinking by people who have never been taught how to weigh evidence, reach conclusions, or recognize the flaws in their own reasoning; the astonishing decline in literacy; loneliness; the sense of purposelessness that marks so many lives; people who don’t understand themselves or one another. How sick does a civilization have to be to not pass down its own sources of wisdom and meaning to its children?
paragraphs.zh: 由此带来的文明与道德知识的流失已产生了现实后果：那些从未被教导过如何权衡证据、得出结论，以及如何识别自己推理中的缺陷的人，进而陷入松散的思维；令人震惊的识字率下降；普遍的孤独；无数人生来都带着的无目的感；以及人们既不了解自己，也不懂彼此。一个文明要有多病，才会不把自己的智慧与意义来源传递给后代？

paragraphs.en: From the September 2023 issue: David Brooks on why Americans are so awful to one another
paragraphs.zh: 引自2023年9月期：大卫·布鲁克斯谈为何美国人彼此如此糟糕

paragraphs.en: Because we have neglected our own humanistic traditions, a growing gap has opened between our scientific, technological, and economic progress on the one hand, and our social, emotional, and spiritual decay on the other. Fixing this problem doesn’t require that we go back and live in monasteries and nunneries. Nor do we have to confine ourselves to, say, the 1930s canon of Western Civ, or the 1950s version of what constitutes high culture.
paragraphs.zh: 由于我们长期忽视了自身的人文传统，社会正在出现一个不断扩大的裂缝：一方面是科学、技术和经济进步，另一方面却是社会、情感和精神的衰败。要解决这个问题，并不要求我们回到修道院或修女院去生活。我们也不必局限于某一种历史版本，比如西方文明史中的1930年代经典教材，或是20世纪50年代所谓“高雅文化”的定义。

paragraphs.en: I agree with the traditionalists that tradition is important, but I don’t think of it as something we need to go back to. Rather, I see it as something that each generation pushes forward. And for this, we need a humanistic renaissance. In schools, universities, and culture at large, we need to focus more explicitly on the big questions of life: What is my purpose? How should the next generation live? What role should beauty play in my life? How do I build a friendship? What do I owe my spouse, my community, my nation? We need to use the best that has been thought and said by all of the great civilizations of the Earth, but especially by Western civilization, which is our own particular home, our core resource while we try to stumble toward a better future.
paragraphs.zh: 我同意传统主义者的观点，即传统很重要，但我不把传统理解为一种必须回到过去的东西。我更认为，它是每一代人都在向前推进的东西。为此，我们需要一场人文复兴。在学校、大学和整个文化领域，我们应更明确地关注人生的终极问题：我的目标是什么？下一代应该如何生活？美在我的生活中应扮演什么角色？我如何建立友谊？我对配偶、社区和国家各有怎样的责任？我们需要借鉴人类所有伟大文明中最卓越的思想和表达，尤其是西方文明中最有价值的部分，因为它是我们独有的精神家园，是我们在艰难摸索更美好未来时最核心的资源。

paragraphs.en: Though Christopher Lasch considered himself to be on the political left, he is sometimes embraced by the traditionalists for his celebration of rootedness, community, and the traditional family, and for his critique of the meritocratic elite. “The populist tradition offers no panacea for all the ills that afflict the modern world,” he wrote. “It asks the right questions, but it does not provide a ready-made set of answers.” The traditionalists have no panaceas either, but they also ask the right questions. They remind us how important it is to embed ourselves and our children within the great humanist conversation that extends back thousands of years. What we should take from the traditionalists is the idea that restoring our society’s connection to its humanistic legacy and long-standing sources of meaning can actually help us better realize the promises of progress.
paragraphs.zh: 尽管克里斯托弗·拉施自认立场偏左，他有时也会被传统主义者接纳，因为他推崇根基、社群与传统家庭，并批评精英主义的功绩论。正如他所写：“民粹主义传统并未为现代世界的诸多弊病提供万能药。”“它提出了正确的问题，却没有给出现成的答案。” 传统主义者同样没有万能药，但他们也提出了正确的问题。他们提醒我们，必须把自己和我们的孩子放回延续了数千年的伟大人文对话之中。我们应当从传统主义者那里汲取的观念，是要恢复社会与其人文传统及长期意义来源的联系，这实际上能帮助我们更好地实现进步的承诺。

paragraphs.en: This article appears in the May 2026 print edition with the headline “History Is Running Backwards.” When you buy a book using a link on this page, we receive a commission. Thank you for supporting The Atlantic.
paragraphs.zh: 这篇文章出现在2026年5月的纸质版中，标题为《History Is Running Backwards》。当你通过本页链接购买书籍时，我们会获得一笔佣金。感谢你支持《大西洋》杂志。
